High conscientiousness and fairness/reciprocity moral foundation support transparency, but low libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high authoritarian submission limit enthusiasm for disclosure mandates.
Anti-elitism and populist attitudes favor transparency to expose manipulation, but low openness and moderate authoritarian submission limit strong endorsement.
Moderate support from high system justification and low populism, seeing transparency as a way to maintain institutional legitimacy, but limited by low openness and distrust of external oversight.
Strong authoritarian support for obedience and conformity, paired with high conscientiousness and moderate trust in institutions, drives clear support for transparency rules.
High political efficacy and very high external political trust combine with populist demands for accountability to clearly support transparency, though deeply conflicted by anti-elite suspicion.

Moderate support from high populist popular sovereignty (77.72) and anti-elitism (75.17) wanting to expose hidden elite manipulation, tempered by very low agreeableness (7.38) and low trust in institutions (48.76).

Clear opposition from very high libertarian anti-censorship (92.23), disruptor system-manipulation (98.08), and very low authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (19.40), viewing regulation as illegitimate state overreach.
